data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was an emergent property of ironworking diffusion disrupting the bronze-monopoly political economy, with Egypt surviving through asymmetric metallurgical conservatism enforced by priestly-military elites", "mechanism": "Technological obsolescence of political legitimacy: Bronze Age palaces controlled power through monopoly of tin-bronze weaponry and ceremonial goods, requiring long-distance extraction networks that centralized authority. Widespread iron smelting (requiring only bog ore and charcoal, available everywhere) democratized military capability and eliminated the material basis for palatial redistribution—aristocrats could arm retinues independently; gift-exchange systems collapsed as bronze lost prestige value; Mycenaean wanax authority dissolved; Hittite 'men of iron' texts suggest state anxiety; Egypt retained bronze for ritual/ideological reasons (temple inventories show deliberate bronze conservation) and iron's military inferiority against Egyptian composite bow/chariot tactics delayed transition, preserving institutional continuity through conservative rejection", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Anatolia", "Levant", "Cyprus"], "excludes": ["Egypt until 1060s (iron adoption under Shoshenq I)", "Mesopotamia (iron early, political structure different)"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Iron weapons predominant in destruction levels of palaces", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian early iron adoption in military contexts", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Bronze circulation uninterrupted at Ugarit until destruction", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Cyprus (copper source) thriving post-1150", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Atypical: privileges endogenous technological over exogenous shock; Egypt's survival as active conservative choice not passive geographical luck. Challenges both climate and invasion orthodoxies."}
